title: Principles for Reducing the Size of the Federal Reserve's Balance Sheet
summary: The Federal Open Market Committee released principles for significantly reducing the Federal Reserve's balance sheet (QT), with all participants agreeing that the target range for the federal funds rate remains the primary policy adjustment tool and that balance sheet reduction would commence only after the process of raising rates has begun. The Committee intends to reduce holdings primarily by adjusting reinvestment of principal payments received from securities in the System Open Market Account (SOMA) and ultimately intends to hold primarily Treasury securities in the SOMA.
key_points: All FOMC participants agreed on the principles for reducing the balance sheet; vote tally not specified
Target range for federal funds rate remains the PRIMARY means of adjusting monetary policy stance
Balance sheet reduction will commence AFTER the process of increasing the target range has begun
Reduction will occur by adjusting the amounts of principal payments reinvested from SOMA securities holdings
Committee intends to maintain securities holdings in amounts needed to implement policy in an 'ample reserves regime'
In the longer run, Committee intends to hold primarily Treasury securities in SOMA to minimize credit allocation effects
Committee is prepared to adjust approach details in light of economic and financial developments
why_it_matters: This document established the FOMC's formal framework for Quantitative Tightening (QT), providing markets with predictable guidance on how the Fed would reduce its $8-9 trillion balance sheet. It assured markets that rate hikes would precede balance sheet runoff and that the Fed would operate in an 'ample reserves' framework, reducing uncertainty about the pace and mechanics of normalization. This was a key communication for bond market participants, money markets, and any strategy dependent on reserve availability.
macro_context: This document represents the Fed's pre-communication of QT principles ahead of the actual balance sheet reduction that began in June 2022. The sequencing commitment—rates first, then balance sheet runoff—provided a framework for markets to anticipate the dual tightening path. The emphasis on maintaining an ample reserves regime indicated the Fed would be cautious about allowing reserve scarcity that could disrupt money markets.
market_structure_context: The document references the System Open Market Account (SOMA) as the vehicle for holding and reducing securities. The 'ample reserves regime' language indicates the Fed's intention to maintain reserves above the minimum needed for smooth operations. The longer-run intent to hold primarily Treasuries (vs. MBS) addresses how Fed holdings affect credit allocation across sectors of the economy.

issues_found: Source dated January 26, 2022 — approximately 4+ years old relative to TODAY (2026-04-01). Marked as historical record; confidence lowered accordingly.
No specific numbers provided (no pace of reduction, no duration, no dollar amounts for planned runoff). Document contains only qualitative principles.
Source_type is 'press_release' but SOURCE METADATA shows PAGE_TYPE as 'homepage' for navigation context — actual content is a standalone press release.

While relevant as foundational policy communication, the 4+ year time gap from TODAY (2026-04-01) makes this a historical record. notes: This is a historical Federal Reserve policy communication from January 2022. It predates the actual QT implementation that began June 2022 by approximately 5 months. While it establishes the conceptual framework, it lacks the quantitative specifics (pace caps, reinvestment caps) that were later specified in the May 2022 FOMC minutes and subsequent statements. Consider cross-referencing with later QT implementation documentation for operational specifics. The SOURCE METADATA correctly identifies this as relevant to 'macro catalysts' through its policy framework implications.
